none: Swirling white text that reads "Phantom Thread" over a black background.

body.creator: Clara Smith
body.value: The film’s title carries many resonances. It refers to Reynolds’s habit of sewing hidden messages into his creations. It represents Alma herself, a “thread” or element of Reynolds’s life that he can’t fully control. On another level, the film is full of ghosts in the haunted House of Woodcock, Reynolds’s mother foremost among them. In interviews and reviews published around the time of the film’s release, others suggested that the term also refers to a condition seamstresses experienced “after emerging from long, repetitive hours in the workshop,” finding their “hands moving involuntarily, their fingers clasped as though sewing invisible, ‘phantom’ threads” (Bell, “Paul Thomas Anderson on Phantom Thread”).

body.creator: Clara Smith
body.value: The title card’s contrast of a black background with white lettering also thematizes the contrast between the protagonists: highly methodical Reynolds Woodcock and free-spirited Alma Elson. The sound that accompanies the title card with the film’s name resembles auditory feedback (akin to when a microphone is held close to a speaker), which creates a dissonance that foreshadows the narrative development of the film: a man whose life is planned with the utmost care and thrives on routine and orderly surroundings will ultimately enjoy relinquishing control and being defenseless. This sonic dissonance carries a certain degree of irony, since Reynolds does not respond well to input from others, hence any kind of feedback indeed sounds to him as bothersome as this unpleasant sound.

body.creator: Clara Smith
body.value: PTA has denied that the title card is meant to reference that of The Age of Innocence, another film starring Daniel Day-Lewis (Woodward, “Paul Thomas Anderson”).
